answer:
**Iqbal's Attack on Territorial Nationalism in *Wataniyat***  

In the poem *Wataniyat* ("Patriotism"), Allama Iqbal condemns nationalism—understood here as *wataniyat*, a territorial attachment to "land" or nation-state—as a modern Western invention that idolizes division over Islamic unity. He portrays it as the "greatest" of Civilization's new idols, wearing "Faith’s own final desecration," forged by "this new age’s philosophy" to "lay waste the Prophet’s house."[^1] It reduces homeland (*watani*) to mere "land" in secular politics, sowing "rivalry" among peoples, turning trade into "conquest," corrupting politics, oppressing the weak, and severing "Islam’s brotherhood."[^2] True freedom for the Muslim (*Mustafavi*) lies not in territorial confinement, which is a "calamity," but in universal mobility "like a fish at sea," with "homeland... Islam" and "Homeland... by God’s command."[^3]

**Dunoyer's Grounding of Liberty in Race, Climate, and Territory**  

In *Nouveau traité d'économie*, Vol. I (not Vol. II, which applies liberty to industries), Charles Dunoyer argues that human liberty—defined as perfected exercise of faculties through industry and self-regulation—varies by race and external circumstances like climate and territory. Races differ innately in capacity for cultural/liberty-attaining development, with the Caucasian (*homo sapiens europeus*) superior due to cranial/brain structure enabling progressive intelligence.[^4] External conditions (climate, soil, waters, geography) further shape this: favorable positions aid faculty development, while adverse ones hinder, though superior races overcome more.[^5] Peoples must adapt faculties to territorial realities (e.g., nomadism vs. agriculture), with liberty fullest where race and locale align for industrial progress.[^6] Peoples in "favorable" territories (fertile, navigable) develop more if faculties permit, tying liberty to situated, particular development.[^7]

**Exact Point of Iqbal's Argument Cutting Against Dunoyer's**  

Iqbal's core critique—"To be confined to one place is calamity"—directly opposes Dunoyer's premise that liberty emerges from race-specific adaptation *to* territorial/climatic conditions.[^8] Dunoyer roots liberty in differential development *within* delimited locales (e.g., Eskimos vs. Patagonians by stature/climate; Europeans thriving despite "harsh" Europe via racial superiority), implying value in territorial particularism and rivalry (stronger races dominate).[^9] Iqbal rejects this as idolatrous confinement, a "hollow idol" dividing God's creation and Islam's borderless *ummah*; true liberty is trans-territorial witness to the Prophet, unbound by race/climate/land.[^10] Iqbal thus inverts Dunoyer's hierarchy: territorialism (colonial "Nation") enslaves, while universal faith liberates.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 25 (*Wataniyat*), ¶ p-79b1e9, [translation](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-79b1e9); ¶ p-2737d5, [translation](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-2737d5).

[^2]: *Ibid.*, ¶ p-97937b, [translation](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-97937b).

[^3]: *Ibid.*, ¶ p-a723b2, [translation](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-a723b2).

[^4]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, ch. 2 (*I. Influence de la race sur la liberté*), ¶¶ p-f73842, p-4d9a47, p-25e7c5, [translation](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/02-i-influence-de-la-race-sur-la-liberte/translation/#p-f73842).

[^5]: *Ibid.*, ch. 3 (*II. Influence des circonstances extérieures sur la liberté*), ¶¶ p-757cb4, p-881a82, [translation](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/03-ii-influence-des-circonstances-exterieures-sur-la-/translation/#p-757cb4).

[^6]: *Ibid.*, ch. 3, ¶¶ p-0e94e3, p-91e1c1, [translation](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/03-ii-influence-des-circonstances-exterieures-sur-la-/translation/#p-0e94e3).

[^7]: *Ibid.*, ch. 3, ¶ p-27207a, [translation](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/03-ii-influence-des-circonstances-exterieures-sur-la-/translation/#p-27207a).

[^8]: Iqbal, *Wataniyat*, ¶ p-a723b2, [translation](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-a723b2).

[^9]: Dunoyer, *VOL I*, ch. 2, ¶¶ p-5e10b2, p-63bfd3; ch. 3, ¶¶ p-77990a, p-decf84, [translation](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/02-i-influence-de-la-race-sur-la-liberte/translation/#p-5e10b2).

[^10]: Iqbal, *Wataniyat*, ¶¶ p-2737d5, p-97937b, [translation](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-2737d5).
